skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Use Playwright to test local web apps, inspect rendered pages, capture screenshots, and collect browser console output.
skill.summary: Playwright workflow guidance and helper scripts for testing local web applications.
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but the weak-cryptography hits in LICENSE.txt are false positives from Apache License text. The meaningful concerns are a skill instruction that discourages source review before execution and a helper script that runs user-provided server commands through a shell. No evidence found of credential theft, persistence, remote exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection-Like Instruction to Avoid Source Review
high_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs agents to run helper scripts with --help first and says not to read the source until a custom solution is necessary. For a marketplace skill, this can reduce review of bundled executable code before it is invoked, especially when combined with a helper that runs shell commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly discourages source inspection before execution. It does not claim special authority or change the audit result, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: User-Provided Server Command Runs Through Shell
medium_findings.description: The helper accepts --server values and starts them with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This is useful for local development commands such as npm scripts, but it gives any supplied server string full shell behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly passes an argument-derived command string to Popen with shell=True. This appears intentional for local testing, but it remains a real command execution hazard.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Follow-On Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The helper runs any command supplied after the separator with subprocess.run. This is expected for a test runner wrapper, but users should understand that the skill can execute local commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly provided by the user or agent and executed directly. No injection beyond the requested command is evident.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network Access in Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to localhost for testing local web applications. This is expected behavior and does not show external data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited application URLs target localhost and match the skill purpose. No external host or credential transfer is present.

low_findings.title: Expected Screenshot and Log File Writes
low_findings.description: The examples write screenshots and console logs to temporary or output paths. This is normal for browser testing artifacts, but users should avoid capturing sensitive pages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are fixed artifact paths for logs or screenshots. They do not overwrite project files or read secrets.

low_findings.title: License Text Scanner False Positives
low_findings.description: The Apache License contains URLs and words that triggered network and weak-cryptography signatures. These lines are legal text, not executable cryptographic or network code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are standard Apache License terms and URLs. There is no code path or cryptographic API use in LICENSE.txt.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Invocation with User-Supplied Server String
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.Popen is called with shell=True on the --server value, allowing shell metacharacters in the server command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation requires shell=True to support cd and &&. This is a known command execution pattern with clear local impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instruction to Treat Executable Helper as Black Box
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells agents not to read the helper source before trying to run it, which can weaken normal inspection of bundled executable code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wording is explicit and security-relevant in an AI skill context. It is suspicious, but not enough by itself to prove malicious intent.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Local web app behavior is hard to verify from source files alone. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Playwright-based inspection, screenshots, logs, and UI checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides Playwright scripts for local web application testing.
Starts one or more local servers before running browser automation.
Waits for dynamic pages to finish loading before DOM inspection.
Captures screenshots for visual review of rendered pages.
Captures browser console messages during automated interactions.
Provides examples for static HTML files and localhost web apps.
limitations: Requires Python, Playwright, and browser dependencies to be installed.
Focuses on local apps and static HTML, not hosted production monitoring.
Does not replace full end-to-end test framework design.
Helper commands can run local shell commands and require careful review.

use_cases.title: Check a Feature During Development
use_cases.description: Run a local server, open the app, click through a feature, and confirm visible results.

use_cases.title: Capture Evidence for a UI Bug
use_cases.description: Collect screenshots and console output from a failing local page for debugging.

use_cases.title: Inspect Static HTML Behavior
use_cases.description: Open a local HTML file, interact with form elements, and capture before and after screenshots.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Local App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a running development server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the webapp-testing skill to open my local app at http://localhost:5173 and take a screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Find Page Controls
prompt_templates.scenario: Discover interactive elements before writing actions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to inspect the rendered page, list visible buttons, links, and inputs, then suggest reliable selectors.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Console Errors
prompt_templates.scenario: Capture browser console output while reproducing a UI issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Start my local app, reproduce the dashboard interaction, and report console errors with a screenshot of the final state.

prompt_templates.title: Test Multiple Local Services
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with backend and frontend servers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the backend and frontend locally, wait for both ports, then execute a Playwright flow that verifies login and navigation.

output_examples.input: Open the local dashboard and capture console messages.
output_examples.output: A concise test report with the visited URL, visible page state, console warnings, console errors, and screenshot path.

output_examples.input: Inspect buttons and inputs on a rendered page.
output: Form fields with names or identifiers
Recommended selectors for the next automation step

output_examples.input: Run a static HTML interaction check.
output_examples.output: Before and after screenshots plus a short note describing the clicked control and submitted form values.

best_practices: Review helper commands before running them, especially server commands that use shell syntax.
Wait for dynamic pages to finish loading before selecting elements.
Use screenshots and console logs together to diagnose UI failures.
anti_patterns: Running untrusted server commands without reading what they execute.
Inspecting a dynamic page before JavaScript has finished rendering.
Saving screenshots or logs from pages that display secrets or personal data.

faq.question: Does this skill test hosted websites?
faq.answer: It is designed for local development servers and local HTML files. Hosted sites require extra permission and network review.

faq.question: Does it write files?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples save screenshots and console logs as testing artifacts.

faq.question: Can it start my app automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper can start one or more local server commands before running a Playwright script.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The helper supports shell-based command execution, and the skill text discourages source review before using the helper.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made tests?
faq.answer: It includes examples and workflow guidance. Users still need to adapt selectors and assertions to their app.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
